sections.heading: CommanderGabble: A Universal Attack Against ASR Systems Leveraging Fast Speech
: Security and privacy; $\bullet$ Human-centered computing Ubiquitous and mobile computing;

figures.label: Figure 1
figures.caption: Figure 1: An example to show impact of fast speech on performance of AsR speech recognition.

figures.label: Figure 2
figures.caption: Figure 2: Flowchart of constructing adversarial audio.

figures.label: Figure 3
figures.caption: Figure 3: Pass rates for CVC-contained and CVC-free words.

figures.label: Figure 4
figures.caption: Figure 4:ARPABET vowels in IPA:(1) the vertical axis is mapped by vowel height; (2) the horizontal axis determines vowel backness;(3) where a dot appears,the ones to the right and left represent rounded and unrounded (spread) vowels.

tables.label: Table 1
tables.caption: Table 1: Tested commands for over-the-air attacks.

figures.label: Figure 7
figures.caption: Figure 7: Translation accuracy for adversarial audio (Adv) and normal audio (Nor) files generated with different groups of commands (S, M,L denote short, medium, and long commands respectively,and T represents all commands).

tables.label: Table 2
tables.caption: Table 2: OTW case studies (N and A refer to normal and adversarial audio).

tables.label: Table 6
tables.caption: Table 6: Percentage of adversarial audios that xRef successfully detects when one reference service is introduced (we use Amazon, Google,MS,and IBM to refer to Amazon Transcribe, Google STT, Microsoft Azure STT, and IBM Watson STT, respectively).

tables.label: Table 7
tables.caption: Table 7:Average detection rate for adversarial and normal audios when the number of reference services varies.
